expanded_description: Constructing perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors are cornerstone Euclidean constructions that combine all previously learned skills—copying segments, copying angles, and using compass and straightedge. The perpendicular bisector finds the midpoint of a segment and creates a perpendicular line through it. The angle bisector divides an angle into two congruent angles. Both constructions are essential for triangle centers, circle geometry, and countless geometric proofs.

core_concept.principle: A perpendicular bisector is a line that passes through the midpoint of a segment and is perpendicular to it. An angle bisector is a ray from the vertex that divides the angle into two congruent angles. Both can be constructed using only a compass and straightedge, using the properties of intersecting arcs from equal radii to guarantee both bisection and perpendicularity.
core_concept.explanation: Think of the perpendicular bisector as the line of symmetry of a segment—if you fold the plane along this line, one endpoint lands exactly on the other. The compass creates this symmetry by drawing equal circles centered at both endpoints; their intersections define the perpendicular bisector. The angle bisector is the ray of symmetry of an angle—folding along it makes one side land on the other. The compass finds points equidistant from both sides, and the ray through the vertex and that point bisects the angle.

image.alt: Two panels: (1) Segment AB with perpendicular bisector—a vertical line through the midpoint M, marked with right angle square and congruence ticks on AM and MB. (2) Angle ABC with angle bisector BD—ray from B dividing the angle, marked with matching angle arcs and equal angle labels.
image.caption: Perpendicular Bisector (left): bisects a segment at 90°. Angle Bisector (right): divides an angle into two congruent angles.

sections.introduction: The perpendicular bisector of a segment is the line consisting of all points equidistant from the two endpoints. It bisects the segment at its midpoint and is perpendicular to it. The construction uses equal radii from both endpoints—their intersecting arcs locate points equidistant from both ends, which define the perpendicular bisector.

introduction_image.alt: Segment AB with endpoints labeled. Two large overlapping circles of equal radius centered at A and B, intersecting at points P (above) and Q (below). The line PQ passes through the midpoint M of AB and is perpendicular to AB. Congruence marks on AM and MB, right angle square at M.
introduction_image.caption: Equal circles centered at A and B intersect at P and Q. Line PQ is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

key_concept.definition: The perpendicular bisector of segment AB is constructed by drawing two circles of equal radius centered at A and B. Their two intersection points determine a line that passes through the midpoint of AB and is perpendicular to AB. Every point on this line is equidistant from A and B.
key_concept.context: The perpendicular bisector is essential for finding the circumcenter of a triangle (the center of the circumscribed circle), constructing right angles, finding the midpoint of a segment without measurement, and creating lines of symmetry.

image.alt: Step-by-step construction: (1) Set compass to any radius greater than half of AB. (2) Draw arcs from A above and below AB. (3) With same radius, draw arcs from B intersecting the previous arcs at P and Q. (4) Draw line PQ. Labeled: M is midpoint, right angle marked, AM=MB, AP=BP=AQ=BQ.
image.caption: Perpendicular bisector construction: equal radii from A and B create intersecting arcs at P and Q.

process_flow.description: Set the compass to a radius greater than half the length of segment AB. The radius must be large enough that circles centered at A and B will intersect.
process_flow.mechanism: Place the compass anchor on A and extend the pencil so the opening is clearly more than half of AB. Visually, the radius should be about 3/4 of AB or more. The exact radius does not matter as long as it is greater than AB/2 and the same for both circles.
process_flow.result: A compass set to a radius r > AB/2, ready to draw intersecting circles.
applications: If AB is 8 cm, set compass to any radius > 4 cm (e.g., 5 or 6 cm).
The radius must be the SAME for both circles—do not change the compass setting.
If the radius is too small, the circles won't intersect—increase and try again.

image.alt: Segment AB with compass shown. Radius set to a length clearly greater than half of AB. A visual comparison: half of AB marked, compass opening shown to be larger. Label: 'Radius > AB/2 — same setting for both circles.'
image.caption: Step 1: Set compass radius greater than half the segment length. Keep this setting.

process_flow.process: Draw Intersecting Arcs from Both Endpoints
process_flow.description: With the set radius, draw arcs from A above and below the segment. Without changing the compass, draw arcs from B that intersect the arcs from A.
process_flow.mechanism: Anchor compass at A. Draw an arc above AB (imagine a semicircle crossing above the segment) and an arc below AB. Move compass to B—do not change the radius. Draw arcs above and below AB. The arcs from A and B should intersect at two points: one above the segment (call it P) and one below (call it Q).
process_flow.result: Two intersection points P (above) and Q (below) that are both equidistant from A and B.
applications: If arcs do not intersect, the radius was too small—increase and repeat.
Draw the arcs large enough to clearly intersect—faint arcs make points hard to locate.
You only need the arcs near where they cross, not full circles (though full circles work too).

image.alt: Segment AB with arcs drawn. Arcs from A curve above and below. Arcs from B (same radius) curve above and below, intersecting A's arcs at P (above) and Q (below). Intersection points highlighted. Compass shown anchored at B after being at A, same opening.
image.caption: Step 2: Draw arcs from A, then from B with the same radius. Intersections at P and Q.

process_flow.description: Use the straightedge to draw the line through points P and Q. This line is the perpendicular bisector of AB.
process_flow.mechanism: Align the straightedge with points P and Q. Draw a line passing through both. This line will cross AB at its exact midpoint M, forming right angles. Mark the midpoint M where the line crosses AB. Add congruence marks on AM and MB and a right angle square at M.
process_flow.result: Line PQ is the perpendicular bisector. M is the midpoint. PQ ⊥ AB. AM = MB.
applications: The line through P and Q is the set of ALL points equidistant from A and B.
M is the midpoint—no measurement needed to locate it.
Any point on line PQ is the same distance from A as from B.

image.alt: Completed construction: Line PQ drawn through the two intersection points, crossing AB at M. M labeled as midpoint. AM and MB marked with matching tick marks. Right angle square at M. Label: 'PQ is the perpendicular bisector of AB.'
image.caption: Step 3: Draw line PQ. It crosses AB at midpoint M, forming right angles.

classifications.value: Points P and Q are each equidistant from A and B because they lie on circles of equal radius centered at A and B.
characteristics: PA = PB because P lies on a circle of radius r centered at A AND a circle of radius r centered at B — so PA = r = PB
Similarly, QA = QB
Any point equidistant from A and B lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB
Therefore, line PQ is the perpendicular bisector
classifications.behavior: The construction creates two points (P and Q) that are each equidistant from A and B. Two points determine a line. This line is precisely the set of all points equidistant from A and B—which is the definition of the perpendicular bisector.

examples.explanation:
By construction, PA = PB = r and QA = QB = r.

So P and Q are both equidistant from A and B.

In geometry, the set of all points equidistant from A and B is exactly the perpendicular bisector of AB.

Since P and Q are two distinct points on that line, line PQ IS the perpendicular bisector.

image.alt: Diagram with segment AB and perpendicular bisector PQ. Radii labeled: PA = PB = r (blue), QA = QB = r (red). Note: 'P and Q are both equidistant from A and B. The line through them is the perpendicular bisector.'
image.caption: Proof: P and Q are each equidistant from A and B, so line PQ is the perpendicular bisector.

classifications.value: Every point on the perpendicular bisector of AB is equidistant from A and B. Conversely, any point equidistant from A and B lies on the perpendicular bisector.
characteristics: This is an 'if and only if' relationship: point is on perpendicular bisector ↔ point is equidistant from endpoints
Used to find centers of circles passing through given points
Used to construct the circumcenter of a triangle (intersection of perpendicular bisectors of sides)
The shortest path from a point to two other points (via the perpendicular bisector) has equal distances
classifications.behavior: The perpendicular bisector is the line of symmetry for segment AB. If you fold the plane along this line, A lands on B. This symmetry is the geometric meaning of 'equidistant.'

examples.explanation:
To find a point equidistant from A and B: construct the perpendicular bisector of AB. Any point on it works.

To find a point equidistant from THREE points A, B, C: construct perpendicular bisectors of AB and BC. Their intersection is equidistant from all three—this is the circumcenter of triangle ABC.

image.alt: Left: Segment AB with perpendicular bisector. Several points on the line marked with equal distances to A and B. Right: Triangle ABC with perpendicular bisectors of all three sides intersecting at the circumcenter O. OA = OB = OC = circumradius.
image.caption: Every point on the perpendicular bisector is equidistant from the endpoints.

image.alt: Complete construction diagram: Segment AB with perpendicular bisector PQ. Midpoint M marked. Right angle square. Congruence marks on AM and MB. Equal radii shown in dashed lines. Summary of key properties listed.
image.caption: Perpendicular Bisector Construction: method, result, and key properties.

sections.introduction: The angle bisector is a ray from the vertex that divides the angle into two congruent angles. The construction uses an arc centered at the vertex to mark equal distances on both sides, then finds a point equidistant from those two points—which lies on the angle bisector.

introduction_image.alt: Angle ABC with vertex B. An arc from B intersects both sides at X and Y. Two equal-radius arcs from X and Y intersect at point D inside the angle. Ray BD is drawn. The two resulting angles ∠ABD and ∠DBC are marked with matching arcs and labeled congruent.
introduction_image.caption: An arc from the vertex and two equal arcs from the sides locate a point D on the angle bisector.

key_concept.definition: The angle bisector of ∠ABC is constructed by drawing an arc from vertex B intersecting both sides at points X and Y, then drawing equal-radius arcs from X and Y that intersect at point D inside the angle. Ray BD is the angle bisector, and ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC.
key_concept.context: The angle bisector is essential for constructing the incenter of a triangle (center of the inscribed circle), dividing angles into equal parts, and creating lines equidistant from two intersecting lines. It is the angle counterpart to the perpendicular bisector of a segment.

image.alt: Step-by-step construction: (1) Arc from B intersecting BA at X and BC at Y. (2) Compass set to any convenient radius. (3) Arcs from X and Y (same radius) intersecting at D. (4) Ray BD drawn. Result: ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC. Congruence arcs marked.
image.caption: Angle bisector construction: arc from vertex, then equal arcs from side intersections locate the bisector.

process_flow.process: Draw an Arc from the Vertex
process_flow.description: Place the compass anchor at the vertex B. With any convenient radius, draw an arc that intersects both sides of the angle.
process_flow.mechanism: Set the compass to any comfortable radius—large enough to clearly cross both sides. Anchor at B. Draw an arc from one side to the other. The arc intersects side BA at point X and side BC at point Y. These two points are equidistant from B (both equal to the chosen radius).
process_flow.result: Points X and Y on the two sides, with BX = BY = r₁ (the chosen radius).
applications: Choose a radius that clearly crosses both sides—not too small.
BX and BY are equal because they are radii of the same circle centered at B.
Points X and Y will be the centers for the next arcs.

image.alt: Angle ABC with vertex B. An arc centered at B sweeps across both sides, intersecting BA at X and BC at Y. Radii BX and BY are marked equal. Label: 'Arc from B with any radius → BX = BY = r₁.'
image.caption: Step 1: Draw an arc from the vertex intersecting both sides at X and Y.

process_flow.process: Draw Equal Arcs from X and Y
process_flow.description: Without necessarily keeping the original radius, set the compass to any convenient radius. Draw arcs from X and Y that intersect each other inside the angle.
process_flow.mechanism: Set the compass to a radius large enough that arcs from X and Y will intersect inside the angle. Anchor at X, draw an arc in the interior of the angle. Without changing the radius, anchor at Y and draw another arc in the interior. The two arcs should intersect at a point—call it D.
process_flow.result: Point D inside the angle, with XD = YD = r₂ (the second chosen radius).
applications: The radius for arcs from X and Y can be different from the first radius r₁.
It must be large enough that the arcs intersect inside the angle.
Do not change the radius between drawing from X and drawing from Y.

image.alt: Angle ABC with X and Y marked. Arcs drawn from X and from Y with equal radius r₂. The arcs intersect at point D in the interior of the angle. Label: 'Arcs from X and Y with same radius r₂ → XD = YD = r₂.'
image.caption: Step 2: Draw equal-radius arcs from X and Y, intersecting at point D.

process_flow.description: Use the straightedge to draw a ray from vertex B through point D. This ray is the angle bisector.
process_flow.mechanism: Place the straightedge so it passes through B and D. Draw a ray starting at B, passing through D, and extending beyond. This ray BD divides ∠ABC into two congruent angles: ∠ABD and ∠DBC.
process_flow.result: Ray BD is the angle bisector. ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC. Each is half of ∠ABC.
applications: Mark matching arcs on ∠ABD and ∠DBC to indicate congruence.
Every point on ray BD is equidistant from the two sides of the angle.
The bisector is the line of symmetry of the angle.

image.alt: Completed construction: Ray BD drawn from B through D. ∠ABD and ∠DBC marked with matching congruence arcs. Point D shown as intersection of arcs. Label: 'Ray BD bisects ∠ABC → ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC.'
image.caption: Step 3: Draw ray BD. It bisects the angle into two congruent angles.

classifications.value: The construction creates two congruent triangles (ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD) by SSS, proving that ∠XBD ≅ ∠YBD, which means ray BD bisects ∠ABC.
characteristics: BX = BY (same radius r₁ from arc centered at B)
XD = YD (same radius r₂ for arcs from X and Y)
BD = BD (common side — the ray itself)
By SSS: ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD
Therefore, ∠XBD ≅ ∠YBD, which are ∠ABD and ∠DBC
classifications.behavior: The construction secretly builds two triangles with three equal sides. SSS congruence guarantees all corresponding parts are equal, including the angles at B. This is a physical SSS proof that the ray bisects the angle.

examples.process: The SSS proof in the angle bisector construction
examples.explanation:
Triangle BXD: sides BX (= r₁), XD (= r₂), and BD (common).

Triangle BYD: sides BY (= r₁), YD (= r₂), and BD (common).

BX = BY, XD = YD, BD = BD → ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD by SSS.

Corresponding angles ∠XBD and ∠YBD are congruent.

These are exactly ∠ABD and ∠DBC. So BD bisects ∠ABC.

image.alt: Triangles BXD and BYD highlighted in different colors. Corresponding sides marked: BX=BY (r₁, red), XD=YD (r₂, blue), BD=BD (common, green). SSS congruence shown. Label: 'ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD → ∠XBD ≅ ∠YBD → BD bisects ∠ABC.'
image.caption: Proof: ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD by SSS, so corresponding angles are equal—BD bisects the angle.

classifications.type: The Equidistant Property of Angle Bisectors
classifications.value: Every point on the angle bisector is equidistant from the two sides of the angle. Conversely, any point in the interior equidistant from both sides lies on the angle bisector.
characteristics: Distance from a point to a line is measured perpendicular to the line
Points on the bisector have equal perpendicular distances to both sides
This property is used to construct the incenter of a triangle
The incenter is the intersection of angle bisectors and is the center of the inscribed circle
classifications.behavior: The angle bisector is the line of symmetry for the angle. If you fold the plane along the bisector, one side of the angle lands exactly on the other side. Any point on the bisector is the same perpendicular distance from both sides.

examples.explanation:
Construct the angle bisectors of all three angles of a triangle.

They intersect at exactly one point: the incenter I.

I is equidistant from all three sides of the triangle.

A circle centered at I with that distance as radius is tangent to all three sides—the inscribed circle (incircle).

image.alt: Triangle with all three angle bisectors drawn, intersecting at incenter I. Perpendicular distances from I to each side shown equal (dashed lines). Incircle drawn centered at I, tangent to all three sides.
image.caption: Angle bisectors of a triangle meet at the incenter, center of the inscribed circle.

image.alt: Complete construction diagram: Angle ABC with bisector BD. Points X and Y on sides. Point D from intersecting arcs. Congruence marks on angles. SSS triangles highlighted. Summary of key properties.
image.caption: Angle Bisector Construction: method, result, and key properties.

sections.introduction: Perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors are conceptually parallel constructions. The perpendicular bisector finds the midpoint of a segment; the angle bisector finds the midline of an angle. Both use intersecting equal-radius arcs to locate points with a symmetry property.

introduction_image.alt: Side-by-side comparison: Left—Perpendicular bisector of segment AB, arcs from A and B intersect at P and Q. Right—Angle bisector of ∠ABC, arcs from X and Y intersect at D. Both use equal radii from two points to find a point on the bisector.
introduction_image.caption: Both bisectors use intersecting equal-radius arcs to find points with a symmetry property.

key_concept.definition: The perpendicular bisector construction uses equal circles centered at the segment's endpoints. The angle bisector construction uses equal arcs from points on the angle's sides. Both find points equidistant from two reference objects—endpoints for the perpendicular bisector, sides for the angle bisector.
key_concept.context: Understanding the parallel structure helps remember the steps: both constructions use a compass to mark equal distances, then find the intersection of equal-radius arcs drawn from those marked points. The symmetry of the construction guarantees the bisection property.

image.alt: Comparison table: Perpendicular Bisector — Reference: two endpoints A and B, Step 1: equal circles from A and B, Result: line perpendicular through midpoint. Angle Bisector — Reference: vertex B and sides, Step 1: arc from vertex to mark X and Y, Step 2: equal arcs from X and Y, Result: ray bisecting the angle.
image.caption: Parallel constructions: equal distances create symmetry, yielding bisection.

classifications.value: A structural comparison of the two most important bisector constructions.
characteristics: Perpendicular Bisector: Reference objects are two points (endpoints A and B)
Angle Bisector: Reference objects are two lines (sides of the angle)
Perpendicular Bisector: One compass setting (radius > AB/2), used from both endpoints
Angle Bisector: Two compass settings (first radius r₁ for arc from vertex, second radius r₂ for arcs from X and Y)
Perpendicular Bisector: Result is a LINE (infinite, perpendicular to segment)
Angle Bisector: Result is a RAY (from vertex, inside angle)
classifications.behavior: Both produce symmetry: the perpendicular bisector is the line of symmetry for a segment; the angle bisector is the line of symmetry for an angle. Both are unique for their given objects.

examples.process: Decision flowchart: which bisector to construct?
examples.explanation:
Question: 'Am I bisecting a SEGMENT or an ANGLE?'

Segment → Perpendicular Bisector: use equal circles from both endpoints.

Angle → Angle Bisector: use arc from vertex, then equal arcs from side intersections.

The name tells you the reference object: 'perpendicular bisector' bisects a segment; 'angle bisector' bisects an angle.

image.alt: Flowchart: 'What am I bisecting?' → 'A SEGMENT' → 'Perpendicular Bisector: equal circles from endpoints'. → 'An ANGLE' → 'Angle Bisector: arc from vertex, then equal arcs from side intersections'. Each path with a small diagram.
image.caption: Decision guide: the object being bisected determines which construction to use.

image.alt: Unified comparison diagram: Both constructions shown with corresponding elements highlighted. Perpendicular bisector on the left, angle bisector on the right. Labels connecting equivalent steps: 'equal radii' → 'equal radii', 'intersection points' → 'intersection point', 'line through points' → 'ray through point'.
image.caption: Both bisectors use the same geometric principle: equal distances create symmetry.

sections.introduction: Bisector constructions require attention to compass settings and arc intersections. Errors often come from using a radius too small, changing compass settings inadvertently, or confusing the two types of bisectors.

key_concept.definition: Common errors in bisector constructions include: using a radius too small for arcs to intersect, changing the compass setting between drawing the two equal-radius arcs, confusing perpendicular bisector steps with angle bisector steps, and forgetting to verify the construction with congruence marks.
key_concept.context: These errors produce lines that look approximately correct but are geometrically inaccurate. A construction must be exact—the intersection points must be precisely defined by equal radii.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) Arcs from A and B that don't intersect — 'Radius too small'. (2) Compass with changed setting — 'Keep same radius for paired arcs'. (3) Confused diagram mixing perpendicular bisector and angle bisector elements. (4) Construction without verification marks — 'Always mark congruence'.
image.caption: Common bisector errors: small radius, changed settings, confusing the two types, and missing verification.

classifications.value: In perpendicular bisector: radius ≤ half of AB, so circles don't intersect. In angle bisector: arcs from X and Y don't reach each other.
characteristics: Perpendicular bisector: radius MUST be > AB/2 for circles to intersect
Angle bisector: radius for arcs from X and Y must be large enough to meet inside the angle
If arcs don't intersect, increase the radius and try again
classifications.behavior: The intersection points are essential—they define the bisector. Without intersection, the construction cannot proceed. Always test that the compass setting produces intersecting arcs before committing to the final drawing.

examples.explanation:
Perpendicular bisector: If arcs from A and B don't meet, the radius is ≤ AB/2. Increase the compass opening to about 3/4 of AB.

Angle bisector: If arcs from X and Y don't meet inside the angle, increase the second radius r₂. Make it large enough that the arcs clearly cross.

image.alt: Two error diagrams: (1) Perpendicular bisector attempt with radius = AB/2, circles touch at one point only (tangent). (2) Angle bisector with arcs from X and Y too small, not intersecting. Both corrected with larger radii shown in green.
image.caption: Arcs must intersect. Increase radius until they clearly cross.

classifications.note: Solution: For perpendicular bisector, use a radius noticeably greater than half the segment. For angle bisector, the second radius (from X and Y) should be generous—it has no upper limit other than practicality.

classifications.type: Changing the Compass Setting Between Paired Arcs
classifications.value: Using one radius for the arc from A and a different radius for the arc from B. Or using different radii for arcs from X and Y.
characteristics: Error: The two arcs meant to intersect must have the SAME radius
If radii differ, the intersection point is NOT equidistant from the two reference objects
Result: The constructed line does not truly bisect
classifications.behavior: The proof of correctness depends entirely on equal radii. Different radii mean the triangles formed are not congruent, the points are not equidistant, and the line is not a true bisector.

examples.explanation:
Perpendicular bisector: If circle from A has radius r₁ and circle from B has radius r₂ ≠ r₁, their intersections are NOT equidistant from A and B. The line through them is NOT the perpendicular bisector.

Angle bisector: If arc from X has different radius than arc from Y, point D is not equidistant from X and Y, and BD is not the angle bisector.

image.alt: Two diagrams: (1) Circles centered at A and B with clearly different radii (one large, one small). Intersections marked with question marks. (2) Arcs from X and Y with different radii. Result labeled 'NOT a bisector'.
image.caption: Unequal radii destroy the construction. Paired arcs must use the exact same compass setting.

classifications.note: Solution: For perpendicular bisector, set the radius once and do not change it when moving from A to B. For angle bisector, after drawing the first arc from the vertex, set a new radius for arcs from X and Y—and do not change it between X and Y.

classifications.type: Confusing Perpendicular Bisector and Angle Bisector Steps
classifications.value: Mixing elements of the two constructions, such as drawing arcs from endpoints to bisect an angle, or drawing an arc from the vertex to bisect a segment.
characteristics: Error: Applying segment bisector logic (circles from endpoints) to an angle
Error: Applying angle bisector logic (arc from vertex, then arcs from side points) to a segment
Correct: Match the construction to the object being bisected
classifications.behavior: The two constructions are structurally similar but applied to different objects. Confusing them produces nonsense—a line that bisects neither a segment nor an angle.

examples.process: Correct identification of the construction needed
examples.explanation:
'Bisect segment AB' → Perpendicular Bisector: compass on A and B, equal circles, line through intersections.

'Bisect ∠ABC' → Angle Bisector: compass on B, arc to sides. Then compass on X and Y, equal arcs, ray through intersection.

image.alt: A confused diagram with a segment and an angle merged. Red X over the whole thing. Below: clean separation showing the correct construction for each. Label: 'Segment → Perpendicular Bisector. Angle → Angle Bisector. Do not mix.'
image.caption: Keep the constructions distinct. Segment bisector ≠ Angle bisector.

classifications.note: Solution: Before starting, state: 'I am bisecting a [segment/angle].' This primes your brain for the correct procedure. The name of the construction tells you exactly what to do.

worked_examples.title: Diagnosing Bisector Construction Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student attempts to construct the perpendicular bisector of segment PQ but the resulting line does NOT pass through the midpoint of PQ. Another student's angle bisector of ∠XYZ does NOT divide the angle into two equal-looking parts. Diagnose possible errors.

1) What are two likely errors for the perpendicular bisector failure?

2) What are two likely errors for the angle bisector failure?

step_by_step.step: 1a) Perpendicular bisector error: radius too small
step_by_step.explanation: If the compass radius was ≤ PQ/2, the arcs from P and Q either didn't intersect or just barely touched. The student may have forced a line through unclear intersection points, resulting in an inaccurate line.

If the student changed the compass setting between drawing from P and drawing from Q, the intersection points are not equidistant from P and Q. The line through them is not the perpendicular bisector.

step_by_step.step: 2a) Angle bisector error: different radii for arcs from X and Y
step_by_step.explanation: After marking X and Y on the sides, the arcs from X and Y must have the SAME radius. If the student changed the setting between them, point D is not equidistant from X and Y, and ray BD does not bisect the angle.

step_by_step.step: 2b) Angle bisector error: radius for arcs from X and Y too small
step_by_step.explanation: If the second radius was too small, the arcs from X and Y didn't intersect inside the angle. The student may have guessed at point D rather than finding the true intersection.

image.alt: Four panels showing each error: (1a) Arcs just touching, unclear intersection. (1b) Circles with different radii. (2a) Arcs from X and Y with mismatched radii. (2b) Arcs from X and Y too small to meet. Each with the correct version shown for comparison.
image.caption: Diagnosing bisector errors: radius too small, unequal radii, or forced intersections.

worked_examples.final_answer:
Perpendicular bisector failures: (1) radius ≤ half the segment, arcs don't intersect; (2) compass setting changed between endpoints, so radii unequal.

Angle bisector failures: (1) second radius changed between arcs from X and Y; (2) second radius too small for arcs to intersect inside the angle.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Both bisectors depend on equal radii producing clean intersections. Changing settings or using too-small radii produces inaccurate results.

sections.introduction: Mastery of bisector constructions requires hands-on practice and the ability to describe both the steps and the geometric reasoning behind them. Work through these problems to build precision and understanding.

worked_examples.title: Bisector Construction Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Construct the perpendicular bisector of a segment AB of length 10 cm. Describe each step and verify that M is the midpoint.

2) Construct the angle bisector of a 60° angle. Describe each step and explain why the construction works in terms of triangle congruence.

3) Given segment XY, explain how the perpendicular bisector construction locates the midpoint without measurement.

4) Why must the second radius in the angle bisector construction be the same for arcs from both X and Y?

step_by_step.step: 1) Construct perpendicular bisector of 10 cm segment
step_by_step.explanation:
1. Set compass to radius > 5 cm (e.g., 6 or 7 cm).

2. Anchor at A, draw arcs above and below AB.

3. Without changing radius, anchor at B, draw arcs intersecting those from A at P and Q.

4. Draw line PQ. It crosses AB at midpoint M. AM = MB = 5 cm.

5. Verify: Measure AM and MB—both should be 5 cm. PQ should be perpendicular (check with right angle square).

1. Compass on vertex B, draw arc intersecting sides at X and Y.

2. Set compass to convenient radius. Anchor at X, draw arc in interior.

3. Same radius, anchor at Y, draw arc intersecting first at D.

4. Draw ray BD. ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC, each 30°.

Proof: BX = BY (arc 1 radii). XD = YD (arc 2 radii, same setting). BD = BD (common). ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD by SSS. So ∠XBD ≅ ∠YBD → BD bisects ∠ABC.

step_by_step.step: 3) How midpoint is found without measurement
step_by_step.explanation: The construction uses only the compass to capture the length of AB indirectly. The intersection points P and Q are each equidistant from A and B because they lie on circles of equal radius centered at A and B. The line PQ is the perpendicular bisector, and its intersection M with AB is exactly the midpoint—by the symmetry of the construction, M divides AB into two equal parts. No ruler measurement is needed.

step_by_step.step: 4) Why second radius must be equal for both arcs
step_by_step.explanation: In the SSS proof, XD must equal YD. If different radii are used for the arcs from X and Y, then XD ≠ YD. The triangles ΔBXD and ΔBYD would NOT be congruent. Without congruence, the corresponding angles ∠XBD and ∠YBD would not be proven equal, and BD would not be guaranteed to bisect the angle. The equal radius ensures the third side of the SSS proof.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Completed perpendicular bisector of 10 cm segment with midpoint verified. (2) Completed angle bisector of 60° angle, each half 30°, with SSS proof highlighted. (3) Diagram showing midpoint found without ruler. (4) Diagram contrasting equal vs. unequal radii for angle bisector.
image.caption: Bisector construction practice: performing and explaining the reasoning behind the steps.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Perpendicular bisector constructed. M is midpoint: AM = MB = 5 cm. PQ ⊥ AB.

2) Angle bisector constructed. ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC = 30°. Proof: ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD (SSS).

3) The midpoint is the intersection of the perpendicular bisector with the segment, located by symmetry from equal circles—no ruler needed.

4) Equal radii ensure XD = YD, which is required for SSS congruence to prove the angles are equal.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Perpendicular bisector: equal circles from endpoints. Angle bisector: SSS triangle congruence from equal arcs. Both use symmetry to achieve exact bisection without measurement.

summary: Constructing perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors are foundational Euclidean constructions that use intersecting equal-radius arcs to create symmetry. Key points: 1) Perpendicular Bisector: Set compass radius greater than half the segment. Draw equal circles from both endpoints. Their intersection points define a line that passes through the midpoint and is perpendicular to the segment. Proof: Both intersection points are equidistant from the endpoints, so the line through them is the perpendicular bisector. Every point on this line is equidistant from the segment's endpoints. 2) Angle Bisector: Draw an arc from the vertex intersecting both sides at X and Y. Set a new compass radius. Draw equal arcs from X and Y intersecting at D inside the angle. Ray BD bisects the angle. Proof: BX = BY (first radii), XD = YD (second radii), BD = BD → ΔBXD ≅ ΔBYD by SSS → corresponding angles are equal → BD bisects ∠ABC. 3) Both constructions rely on the same principle: equal distances create symmetry, and symmetry guarantees bisection. 4) Common errors: Radius too small (arcs don't intersect), changing compass setting between paired arcs (destroys equality), confusing the two constructions, and failing to verify results with congruence marks. 5) Applications: Perpendicular bisectors find triangle circumcenters (centers of circumscribed circles). Angle bisectors find triangle incenters (centers of inscribed circles). Both are essential for advanced geometric constructions.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Bisector Constructions' in center. Branches: Perpendicular Bisector (equal circles from endpoints, intersects at midpoint and 90°, equidistant property, circumcenter application), Angle Bisector (arc from vertex, equal arcs from side points, SSS proof, equidistant from sides, incenter application), Common Principles (equal radii, intersecting arcs, symmetry), Common Errors (small radius, changed settings, confusing constructions).
